title: Workers' Pay Rise: Must It Still Remain a Question Mark?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR-derived text inserted Latin fragments into `2007-2008ஆம் ஆண்டிற்கு` and `பக்கம் பக்கமாக விளம்பரங்களைத் தருவதற்காகவும்`. Both readings were verified from the source pages and restored in the derived Tamil copy. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: The wage agreement for Transport Corporation employees still remains unsettled. Let me now explain the position regarding the Electricity Board's pay rise. For the past two or three days, newspapers have continuously reported how power cuts are increasing. Though newspapers supporting the ruling party try to conceal it, a few journals that uphold journalistic ethics continue to write openly about the situation.

Just as Transport Corporation employees receive a wage-revision agreement once every three years, Electricity Board workers should receive one every four years. Electricity Board LPF General Secretary Singara Rathinasabapathy issued a statement about this two days ago. But the AIADMK government does not appear to have taken any action.

The first wage increase in the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board was granted only after I became Chief Minister in 1969. As Chief Minister, I personally invited Electricity Board trade-union leaders for talks on 25 May 1970. The first wage-revision agreement was then signed on 27 May 1970, with the increase taking effect from 1 December 1969.

The second agreement was signed on 18 February 1977, during the Emergency, and the increase took effect from 1 December 1975. The third and fourth agreements were signed while M. G. R. was Chief Minister. The fifth and seventh were signed while I was Chief Minister.

The seventh wage revision took effect from 1 December 1996. The agreement was signed on 8 July 1998 and granted a forty per cent increase. It also provided one increment to those who had completed seven years of service as of 1 December 1996, two increments after seventeen years, three after twenty-seven years, and four after thirty-seven years.

The eighth wage revision under the AIADMK government should have taken effect from 1 December 2000, but it was postponed by twenty-four months. The agreement was signed only on 15 October 2005. Workers received merely Rs 100 a month for twenty-four months, totalling Rs 2,400.

The ninth wage revision was granted under the DMK government from 1 December 2007. The agreement covered four years and, as under the earlier DMK government, provided a forty per cent increase. Those who had completed fifteen years of service received one increment.
That agreement expired in 2011, and two years have now passed. The Electricity Board should have granted a new wage revision from 1 December 2011 to all 80,000 employees.

On 16 December 2011, the Board appointed a committee headed by its Director of Finance. The committee held talks with all unions on three occasions: 1 September 2012, 28 February 2013 and 27 July 2013. Yet after three rounds, instead of announcing the wage increase, it issued a proposal on 23 July 2013 seeking to raise the workers' workload by about forty per cent and asking them to undertake it. The unions refused.

At about 11 a.m. on 14 November 2013, the Board's negotiating committee called all unions for talks. The Board said it had reduced the proposed workload increase from forty to twenty per cent. It offered all workers a five per cent increase in basic pay from 1 December 2011, and an additional three per cent, treated as one increment, to those who had completed fifteen years of service by 30 November 2011.

The Electricity Board Labour Progressive Federation, Stores Workers' Union, Drivers' Union and every other union opposed the offer and said they would not accept it. They pointed out that in 2007, under the DMK government when I was Chief Minister, workers received a forty per cent wage increase and those completing fifteen years received one increment.

The DMK government changed the wage-agreement cycle in 1989 from once every five years to once every four. But when the AIADMK government granted a six per cent increase in basic pay from 1 December 2002, it again extended the agreement period to five years. In 2007, the DMK government restored the four-year cycle.

That is not all. Under the DMK government, with effect from 1 August 2006, fifty per cent of dearness allowance was merged with basic pay at a cost of Rs 125 crore, benefiting approximately 75,720 Electricity Board employees and 68,518 pensioners.

The Board's golden-jubilee celebration was held under the DMK government at Chennai's Nehru Indoor Stadium on 2 July 2007. To mark the occasion, 145,813 workers, pensioners and family pensioners received a cash gift of Rs 1,000 each, totalling Rs 14,58,13,000.
Every Board worker also received one incentive increment as a special gift from 1 July 2007.
Under the DMK government, 21,401 contract workers and 5,646 part-time workers, a total of 27,047, each received a cash gift of Rs 500, totalling Rs 1,35,23,500.

For 2006–07, 66,066 Electricity Board employees received a twenty per cent bonus and ex gratia payment, comprising an 8.33 per cent bonus and 11.67 per cent ex gratia. Approximately 25,065 contract and part-time workers received Rs 1,000 each as ex gratia. The Board incurred an additional expenditure of Rs 57.95 crore. Another 9,190 officers received Rs 1,000 each, costing the Board Rs 91.90 lakh.

For 2007–08, 69,556 Electricity Board employees received the same twenty per cent bonus and ex gratia, costing Rs 58.43 crore. Approximately 20,387 contract and part-time workers also received Rs 1,000 each.
For 2008–09, 64,772 employees received the twenty per cent bonus and ex gratia, costing Rs 54.41 crore. Approximately 22,018 contract and part-time workers received Rs 1,500 each.
The bonus and ex gratia paid to Board employees and contract workers for 2009–10 cost the Board an additional Rs 60.75 crore.

After the Pay Revision Committee was formed and held talks with the unions, then Electricity Minister Arcot Veerasamy met union representatives on 9 November 2009 and announced terms acceptable to both sides. The pay revision took effect from 1 December 2007. The rates of dearness allowance, house-rent allowance and city compensatory allowance followed those of the Tamil Nadu government. Other special pay and allowances already received by employees were doubled from 1 September 2009.

Under the announcement made by the DMK government, whenever the Tamil Nadu government revises dearness allowance for its employees, the same rate continues to be granted to Board workers, officers and pensioners.

The unions have pointed out that when Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister in 2005, she gave Electricity Board workers only a six per cent wage rise. After the DMK government subsequently granted forty per cent, the LPF says it cannot accept the present offer of five per cent and demands that it be increased.

The LPF has already demanded a thirty per cent wage increase and a twenty per cent increase in other allowances. But the Board has announced only five per cent and said it will not accept even a one-rupee increase in the other allowances. This has shocked and deeply disappointed Electricity Board workers.

While refusing the workers their rightful wage increase, the AIADMK government is said to be collecting security deposits for domestic electricity connections without prior notice and multiplying electricity charges. Newspapers have carried interviews with homemakers about this.

Residents who once paid Rs 100 every two months now say their bills have silently risen to between Rs 300 and Rs 500. Those paying Rs 5,000 every two months are being compelled to pay an additional security deposit of Rs 2,500. They have condemned this.
Why does the government refuse a wage increase to the Electricity Board employees who must collect these additional sums from consumers on its behalf?

The AIADMK government pours out crores of public money for various sporting events and page after page of advertisements, yet Electricity Board workers lament that it strikes at the stomachs of those who genuinely labour.

Workers insist upon at least a twenty-five per cent increase in basic pay and a substantial rise in allowances. Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi leader Thol. Thirumavalavan issued a statement yesterday demanding a twenty-five per cent wage increase.

Not one union that participated in the talks is prepared to accept the five per cent increase offered by the AIADMK government. In this situation, the Tamil Nadu government must recognise that Electricity Board employees are our own people, swiftly conclude the wage agreement with them, and come forward to grant at least a twenty-five per cent increase.
With affection,
M. K.
19 November 2013
